mappings.rationale: Covers branches of public law including constitutional law, criminal law, and judicial procedure.
Directly covers constitutional law governing the organization and functions of the state (subsection A.1).
Covers constitutional law in a parliamentary system, relevant to subsection A.1.
Directly covers laws governing relations among sovereign states including international institutions (subsection B).
Covers the attempt to create supranational legislative and judicial authority (subsections B.2 and B.3).
Covers criminal law principles and the criminal justice system (subsection C).
Covers the study of crime and criminal law from sociological and legal perspectives.
Covers the organization and administration of the judicial system (subsection F.1).
Directly covers the courts, judiciary, and methods of judicial procedure (subsection F).
Covers the jury system as part of judicial procedure and the administration of justice (subsection F).
Covers habeas corpus as a fundamental legal procedure protecting individual rights.
Directly covers laws governing offenses by military forces and persons under military discipline (subsection C.2).
Covers laws promoting public welfare and international human rights protections (subsection D).
Covers laws providing for general social security and welfare (subsection D.1).
Covers laws promoting public health and safety (subsection D.2).
Covers laws relating to health, medical regulation, and patient welfare.
Covers laws regulating the health, safety, and welfare of workers (subsection D.3).
Covers public welfare laws related to environmental protection and public safety.
Directly covers laws governing taxation (subsection E).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Stella Z. Theodoulou, Ravi K. Roy
mappings.rationale: Covers laws governing public administration including regulatory powers and functions (subsection A.2).
Covers the UN as a supranational legislative and executive authority (subsection B.2).
Covers the attempt to punish war crimes and crimes against humanity (subsection B.5).
Covers the foundational principle underlying constitutional and administrative law.
Covers comparisons between common law and civil law systems in criminal and public law.
Covers scientific methods used in criminal investigations and the administration of criminal justice.
Covers psychological methods applied in criminal proceedings and judicial processes.
Covers privacy rights as a matter of public law and constitutional protection.
Covers freedom of speech as a constitutional right and its legal protections.
Covers blasphemy laws as a form of criminal law regulating religious speech.
Directly covers administrative law concepts including judicial review, tribunals, ombudsmen, and public sector accountability, all central to this section on public law.
